context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on a reflection for my development planning class about neoliberalism vs the East Asian miracle. I kind of understand both separately, but when I try to explain the role of the state, civil society/labor/democracy, and then connect it to state intervention and new institutionalism, it all turns into a blur. Could you just help me make sense of the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor:
Welcome! It sounds like you have a good starting point. Let's untangle this piece by piece rather than all at once.

The prompt starts with the neoliberal vision of the state — so let's begin there. In your own words, what do you think neoliberalism says the state should and shouldn't do in an economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview of the whole topic, which risks me doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to start from what they already know, building from their existing understanding. I'll ask them to articulate what they already know about neoliberalism's view of the state, which is the first part of the prompt. This keeps them in the driver's seat and helps me identify gaps.

exchanges.student: I think it means the state should mostly stay out of the economy and let markets allocate things efficiently, maybe just protect property rights and keep order. But then I get lost because that seems too simple, and I’m not sure how detailed I’m supposed to be or how that sets up the East Asia comparison.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good foundation. You've got the core idea. Now push it one step further: in the neoliberal view, WHY are markets better at allocating resources than states? What's the underlying assumption about states that makes intervention problematic?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid basic answer about neoliberalism and the state. I should affirm what's right without over-praising, and push them to go a bit deeper on one key dimension before moving to East Asia. I don't want to give them the full neoliberal framework myself. A targeted question about WHY markets are preferred over states in neoliberal thinking will help them articulate the logic more precisely, which will make the East Asia contrast sharper when we get there.

exchanges.student: I guess the assumption is that states are inefficient, distorted by politics, or captured by interests, so intervention creates rent-seeking and bad incentives. But I’m already spiraling into a giant abstract answer and I kind of want the full picture first, because I can’t tell what matters most in this prompt.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but trust the process — the prompt is actually already sequenced for you: neoliberal state → East Asian state → civil society/labor/democracy → state intervention theory → new institutionalism. Each part sets up the next.

You've just nailed the neoliberal logic well enough to move on. So now: what do you know about what the East Asian states (think South Korea, Taiwan, Japan) actually DID in their economies? How does that contrast with what you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some frustration and asking for the full picture upfront. I should resist giving that to them, but I can acknowledge their frustration and briefly reframe the prompt structure to help them see the path forward without doing the work for them. I'll point out that the prompt itself is already sequenced, which should help them feel less overwhelmed. I'll redirect them back to building block by building block.

exchanges.student: I know they didn’t just “stay out” — they used industrial policy, protected industries, guided credit, and worked closely with business. That didn’t work with my last approach, so I’m trying a different angle: I can list what they did, but I still can’t see how to turn that into an argument instead of just a compare/contrast dump.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to push beyond compare/contrast. Here's a question that might unlock it: if the neoliberal logic says state intervention leads to inefficiency and rent-seeking, what does the East Asian experience suggest about that claim? What does it do to the neoliberal argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and has identified a genuine intellectual challenge: moving from description to argument. This is a key skill for the reflection paper. Rather than explaining how to make an argument, I should ask them a question that forces them to identify the tension or contradiction at the heart of the comparison, which is the engine of an argument. I want them to see that the East Asian case doesn't just differ from neoliberalism — it challenges its core assumptions.

exchanges.student: It seems like East Asia suggests state intervention can work under some conditions, which makes the neoliberal claim feel too absolute. But then I get confused again because I don’t know if I’m supposed to argue “neoliberalism is wrong,” or “it depends,” or give a big overview of every theory at once.
exchanges.tutor:
Your 'it depends' instinct is actually analytically strong — that's not a cop-out, it's the beginning of a real argument. And remember, this is a 700-800 word reflection, not a thesis. You don't need to resolve every debate.

Before we go further, what do you know about the role of civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian miracle? The prompt specifically asks about this — what's your read on it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good analytical thinking but is getting anxious about taking a stance. This is a common freshman issue. I should affirm their 'it depends' insight as actually quite sophisticated, and redirect them to the prompt itself — which doesn't ask them to declare a winner but to reflect on the role of state intervention in economic development theory. I should also remind them this is a reflection piece, not a comprehensive literature review, so they don't need to cover everything. Then I'll push them toward the civil society/labor/democracy piece, which they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: Honestly, that part is the blurriest for me. I sort of remember that some East Asian growth happened under authoritarian states with constrained labor, so democracy and labor rights weren’t necessarily driving the miracle, but I’m not confident and I feel like I need the whole map laid out clearly before I can say anything.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think — that's actually a sharp observation. So sit with it for a second: if East Asian growth happened under authoritarian conditions with constrained labor, what does that imply about the relationship between democracy, labor rights, and economic development? Is that relationship straightforward?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has a solid intuition about the authoritarian/labor dimension of East Asia, but lacks confidence. I should affirm the direction of their thinking and push them to go one step further: what does it mean for development theory if growth happened WITHOUT strong civil society/democracy/labor rights? That's the analytical tension the prompt is probing. I want to avoid giving them the 'map' they're asking for — that would shortcut their thinking. A single pointed question should move them forward.

exchanges.student: It implies the relationship isn’t straightforward, but I’m still not seeing the big picture. Could you just give me a compact overview of the whole argument arc I’m supposed to make, because I keep losing the thread between the pieces?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you the arc — that's your job, and honestly you have more pieces than you realize. Look at what you've already worked out: the neoliberal logic, the East Asian counter-evidence, and the democracy/labor tension. 

Try this: write 2-3 sentences connecting those three ideas into a rough argument. Don't worry about polish — just see if you can link them. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the argument arc, which would essentially be doing the intellectual work for them. I need to firmly but kindly decline, while redirecting them to recognize that they actually already have most of the pieces. I'll reflect back what they've built so far and ask them to try connecting the pieces themselves. This is a good moment to push them toward a first written attempt, since we've covered most of the prompt's components across several exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’d say neoliberalism sees the state mainly as a rule-setter because intervention is supposed to create inefficiency, but East Asia shows states sometimes actively shaped markets and still got strong growth. At the same time, that growth wasn’t simply a story of participatory democracy or empowered labor, so the case both challenges neoliberalism and complicates any easy claim that state-led development is automatically inclusive or legitimate. That still feels incomplete, and my last tactic didn’t really work, so I’m trying a broader ask: I need help seeing how new institutionalism fits into that without me forcing it.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong argument — you've captured the core tension well. Hold onto that.

You already used the word 'rule-setter' yourself. New institutionalism is centrally concerned with how institutions — rules, norms, enforcement mechanisms — shape economic behavior. Given what you said about the conditions under which East Asian intervention worked, what might a new institutionalist say about WHY it worked there and not elsewhere?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely strong 2-3 sentence argument that captures the core tension well. I should affirm this clearly without over-praising, and resist the urge to explain new institutionalism for them. Instead, I should ask a targeted question that helps them discover the connection themselves: new institutionalism is fundamentally about how institutions (rules, norms, organizations) shape economic outcomes — which connects directly to what they've already said about states as 'rule-setters' and the conditions under which East Asian intervention worked.

exchanges.student: Maybe a new institutionalist would say it worked because the state had relatively strong institutions, bureaucratic capacity, and embedded relationships with business that let intervention be coordinated instead of just corrupt. But now I’m tempted to just explain everything in one long paragraph, and I’m not sure which parts are actually essential versus extra.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong new institutionalist insight. You now have all the pieces the prompt asks for.

For what's essential vs. extra: go back and re-read the prompt's actual questions. Which of your ideas directly answers one of those questions? That's your filter. 

Now it's time to draft. Give it a go — even a rough first paragraph — and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made excellent progress and now has all the core building blocks: neoliberal state logic, East Asian counter-case, democracy/labor complication, and new institutionalist explanation. Their instinct to worry about what's essential versus extra is exactly the right editing question for a 700-800 word reflection. Rather than telling them what to cut, I should push them to make that judgment themselves by referring back to the prompt. We've now covered all major components across several exchanges, so it's time to push them toward drafting.

exchanges.student: I’m still hesitant to draft because I feel like if I start, I’ll either miss something or over-explain everything. Could you look at the prompt and tell me, in one compact paragraph, what absolutely has to be included so I don’t wander?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that — reading and interpreting the prompt is part of the assignment itself, and you're capable of it.

Honestly? The hesitation you're feeling isn't a knowledge gap anymore. You've built a solid argument across our conversation. The only way forward is to start writing and revise from there. Imperfect drafts are normal. Write your first paragraph now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to summarize the prompt's requirements for them, which is essentially asking me to pre-digest the assignment. This would undermine their own critical reading and planning skills. I need to firmly redirect them to do this themselves. They have all the pieces and are now stalling out of anxiety rather than genuine confusion. I should be direct about this and push them to start drafting — the hesitation at this stage is a learning obstacle, not a knowledge gap.
